title: Probation officers
description: Counsel and advise individuals with alcohol, tobacco, drug, or other problems, such as gambling and eating disorders. May counsel individuals, families, or groups or engage in prevention programs. Diagnose and treat mental and emotional disorders, whether cognitive, affective, or behavioral, within the context of marriage and family systems. Apply psychotherapeutic and family systems theories and techniques in the delivery of services to individuals, couples, and families for the purpose of treating such diagnosed nervous and mental disorders. Counsel and advise individuals and groups to promote optimum mental and emotional health, with an emphasis on prevention. May help individuals deal with a broad range of mental health issues, such as those associated with addictions and substance abuse; family, parenting, and marital problems; stress management; self-esteem; or aging. (Based on 11 related US occupations.)

tasks.task: Interview clients individually, in families, or in groups, assessing their situations, capabilities, and problems to determine what services are required to meet their needs.
Ask questions that will help clients identify their feelings and behaviors.
Act as liaisons between clients and medical staff, coordinating activities with courts, probation officers, community services, or other post-treatment agencies.

Confer with family members or others close to clients to keep them informed of treatment planning and progress, and counsel clients on concerns such as divorce, child rearing, or financial difficulties.
Follow and evaluate the progress of discharged clients to determine the effectiveness of treatments and counseling processes.
Develop, implement, or evaluate public education, prevention, or health promotion programs, working in collaboration with organizations and communities.
Supervise or direct other workers providing services to clients, and provide public education and consultation regarding counseling services and methods.
Encourage individuals and family members to develop and use skills and strategies for confronting their problems in a constructive manner.
Maintain case files that include activities, progress notes, evaluations, and recommendations, and prepare reports describing client progress.
Collect information about clients using techniques such as testing, interviewing, and observation to develop treatment plans and assess their needs.
Determine whether clients should be counseled or referred to other specialists and confer with clients to develop plans for post-treatment activities.
Conduct prehearing and presentencing investigations, testify in court regarding offenders' backgrounds, and participate in decisions about whether cases should go before courts.

Develop and prepare packets containing information about social service agencies and programs that might be useful for clients or offenders, and refer them to community resources for services like job placement or legal aid.
Counsel clients or patients in individual or group sessions to assist in overcoming dependencies or making life changes, and address their issues related to mental health and substance abuse.
Perform crisis interventions with clients, advocating for them and resolving emergency problems in crisis situations.
Modify treatment plans to comply with changes in client status, and continuously monitor and evaluate client progress according to established goals.
Collaborate with mental health professionals and other staff to perform assessments and develop treatment plans based on the needs of clients.
Provide clients and family members with information about mental health issues and available services, encouraging clients to express their feelings and develop insight.
Plan, organize, or lead structured programs of counseling, work, study, or social activities for clients, and engage in ongoing professional education to improve counseling skills.
Supervise individuals on community-based sentences and monitor their compliance with probation or parole requirements through regular evaluations and visits.
Identify and analyze environmental or personal barriers to client progress through interviews and review of records, and intervene as needed to ensure effective treatment.
Maintain confidentiality of records and prepare all required treatment records and reports promptly and accurately.
Develop and implement individualized treatment plans for clients, addressing their specific needs and goals, and reviewing and adjusting plans as necessary based on client progress.
Collaborate with clients' families and community agencies to implement rehabilitation plans, ensuring that clients receive comprehensive support and resources.
Conduct assessments and evaluations to determine clients' needs and suitability for programs, coordinating treatment with other professionals as required.
Prepare and maintain accurate case histories, evaluate client conditions, and modify treatment activities based on assessments or feedback from other professionals.
Write evaluations and reports for use in court settings, including assessments of clients for suitability for various rehabilitation programs or community services.
Gather information about community mental health needs to better tailor services to meet those needs and support integration of clients into the community.
Discuss with clients their plans for life after treatment, providing guidance and support as they transition back into their daily lives.
Counsel family members to assist them in understanding and supporting clients' treatment processes and goals.

Evaluate clients' physical or mental condition based on review of client information; meet with families, probation officers, police, or other interested parties to exchange necessary information during the treatment process; refer patients, clients, or family members to community resources or to specialists as necessary; educate clients about end-of-life symptoms and options to assist them in making informed decisions.

Guide clients in the development of skills or strategies for dealing with their problems and coping with triggering factors.
Respond to client communications by monitoring voicemail and email, returning phone calls, and making follow-up calls for missed appointments.
